symbols: book or codex in hand — the Gospel of Luke, the written testimony of the evangelist
ox or bull at his feet — the traditional evangelist symbol of Luke, representing sacrifice and priestly service
green robe — life, growth, and the vitality of the Gospel message
arched niche — the honored place of the evangelist in the architecture of the Church
description: A tall, narrow panel painting set within a painted arched niche. A bearded man in a green robe and orange-red mantle stands in a three-quarter view, one hand gesturing toward his chest while the other holds a book or codex. He wears sandals and has a subtle halo. At his feet, partially visible, is a small animal — likely an ox or bull, the traditional symbol of Saint Luke. The background within the niche is a neutral tone, and the overall style is High Renaissance with careful modeling of the drapery and naturalistic proportions.
theological_description: Saint Luke, author of the third Gospel and the Acts of the Apostles, is depicted with his traditional evangelist symbol, the ox. The four evangelist symbols derive from the vision of the four living creatures in Ezekiel 1:10 and Revelation 4:7. The ox was assigned to Luke because his Gospel begins with Zechariah offering sacrifice in the Temple (Luke 1:8-9) — the ox being the primary sacrificial animal — and because Luke's Gospel emphasizes the priestly, sacrificial dimensions of Christ's ministry more than the others. Luke was a physician (Colossians 4:14) and a companion of Paul, and his Gospel is distinguished by its attention to the poor, to women, to Gentiles, and to the universal scope of salvation. His careful historical method is declared in his prologue: 'It seemed good to me also, having followed all things closely for some time past, to write an orderly account' (Luke 1:3). The book he holds represents this written testimony — the inspired record that has transmitted the story of Jesus to all generations.

scripture_refs.reason: Luke's prologue declaring his purpose to write an orderly account, the book he holds in the painting
Paul's identification of Luke as the beloved physician, establishing his identity
The four living creatures including the ox, the source of Luke's evangelist symbol at his feet
Luke's Gospel opens with priestly sacrifice in the Temple, connecting to the ox symbol
